title: KRA to Deploy 72 New Cargo Scanners in Major Customs Modernization Drive
excerpt: The Kenya Revenue Authority is procuring 72 additional cargo scanners for ports, airports, and border crossings, one of the most ambitious upgrades to Kenya's customs infrastructure in recent years.
author: Administrator
body:
The Kenya Revenue Authority (KRA) is set to significantly expand its cargo inspection capabilities, with plans to procure 72 additional scanners for deployment across the country's major ports, airports, and land border crossings.

The move represents one of the most ambitious upgrades to Kenya's customs infrastructure in recent years, with far-reaching implications for trade facilitation, supply chain efficiency, and revenue compliance.

The new equipment will complement KRA's current operational fleet of 33 serviceable scanners already deployed at border stations, airports, and ports nationwide.

The existing network comprises a diverse range of scanning technologies, including fixed and railway scanners, drive-through units, mobile and palletized scanners, as well as baggage inspection systems, reflecting the varied nature of cargo entry points across the country.

The impact of scanner technology on customs operations has been transformative. According to Tabitha Wanyama, KRA's Manager for Customs Warehouse, Customs and Border Control Southern Region, the shift from manual to scanner-based inspection has delivered a dramatic leap in throughput.

For procurement and supply chain professionals, the implications of this investment are significant. Faster cargo clearance translates directly into shorter lead times, reduced warehousing costs, and more predictable supply chain cycles.

KRA has indicated that the expanded scanner network will support faster movement of goods, strengthen revenue collection, and help lower logistics and production costs for businesses operating in and through Kenya.

The procurement drive also signals Kenya's broader intent to tighten enforcement against smuggling, tax evasion, and illicit trade, activities that not only erode government revenue but also distort market conditions and create unfair competition for compliant businesses.

As East Africa's primary trade gateway, Kenya's ability to process cargo efficiently has continental significance, and this investment reinforces the country's commitment to positioning itself as a competitive, transparent, and business-friendly logistics hub.

title: tabb, Kenyan Fintech Launches East Africa's First Large-Scale Fuel Credit Line for Transport and Logistics Firms
excerpt: Kenyan fintech tabb has introduced a structured fuel financing solution for fleet operators and logistics firms, developed in partnership with Galana Energies and operational across its East African service stations.
author: Administrator
body:
Kenyan fintech firm tabb has introduced what it describes as Kenya's first large-scale fuel credit line, offering a structured financing solution for fleet operators, transporters, and logistics companies struggling with the working capital pressures of rising fuel costs.

The facility, developed in partnership with Galana Energies Limited, is already operational across Galana's network of service stations in East Africa.

The model works through a revolving credit line issued by partner banks via tabb's platform. Once approved, businesses can draw on the facility directly at any Galana service station across the region.

The supplier receives immediate payment at the point of sale, while the business repays the bank over an agreed period of between 30 and 90 days, with the credit structured on an interest-free basis.

The launch addresses a financing gap that has long constrained the transport and logistics sector. For many operators, the challenge is not simply the cost of fuel but the significant amount of working capital required upfront to keep fleets running before revenue from deliveries and contracts is received.

The arrangement also eliminates informal supplier credit agreements that have historically strained relationships across the transport value chain.

tabb Director of Mobility Don Okoth framed the launch as a direct expression of the company's market role. "Suppliers get paid on the day. Businesses get the fuel they need. And the informal credit arrangements that have held this sector back begin to give way to something that actually works," he said.

tabb CEO Mesh Alloys described the partnership as a turning point for how the industry finances one of its largest operating expenses. "Credit should be a tool for growth, not a burden, and that is exactly what we are making it," he said, adding that the initiative marks the moment tabb's trade credit network moves from enabling transactions to fundamentally reshaping how the transport and logistics industry accesses finance.

The fuel credit line forms part of tabb's broader strategy to build credit infrastructure across East Africa.

Beyond fuel, the company is expanding its bank-issued credit network into additional sectors including hardware, pharmaceuticals, and retail, positioning itself as a platform that connects banks, suppliers, and businesses to ensure credit flows where it is most needed.

title: LACA Delegation Studies Kenya's Healthcare Supply Chain Model
excerpt: A high-level delegation from the Lagos State AIDS Control Agency toured KEMSA's National Supply Chain Center to study procurement, warehousing, and distribution best practices for HIV commodities.
author: Administrator
body:
In a bid to strengthen Nigeria's healthcare supply chain systems, a high-level delegation from the Lagos State AIDS Control Agency (LASCA) embarked on a strategic study tour to the Kenya Medical Supplies Authority (KEMSA) National Supply Chain Center.

The visit, aimed at tapping into Kenya's proven expertise in health commodity management, focused on critical areas including HIV commodity procurement, warehousing, inventory control, distribution, and overall supply chain optimization.

The delegation sought to adapt best practices that could enhance the efficient delivery of essential health products across Lagos State.

During the engagement, LASCA officials held in-depth discussions with KEMSA experts, exploring robust systems and processes that enable nationwide access to life-saving medicines.

Topics covered included procurement strategies, contract management, performance monitoring, and mechanisms that promote accountability and service excellence.

A major highlight of the tour was KEMSA's innovative approaches to improving Order Fill Rates.

The delegation examined operational measures, key performance indicators, and practical solutions used to consistently meet customer demand and minimize stockouts.

The study tour concluded with a productive exchange of experiences and best practices between the two institutions.

Both sides reaffirmed the value of regional collaboration in building resilient healthcare supply chains and ensuring uninterrupted access to essential health commodities for citizens.

title: Kenya Ports Authority Extends Port of Mombasa Capacity With New Berth 19B Development
excerpt: KPA's Berth 19B extension adds 240 metres of quay length and 300,000 TEUs of annual capacity, part of a strategy to invest in port infrastructure ahead of demand at East Africa's busiest maritime gateway.
author: Administrator
body:
Kenya Ports Authority is pressing ahead with one of its most significant infrastructure developments at the Port of Mombasa, as cargo volumes through East Africa's busiest maritime gateway continue to grow.

At the center of this push is Berth 19B, a major quay extension currently under construction that is designed to keep pace with rising trade demand across the region.

The new berth adds 240 metres of quay length with a draft of 15 metres, built to modern specifications capable of accommodating container vessels of up to approximately 45,000 deadweight tonnage.

The development directly addresses a key operational gap, as larger vessels calling on Mombasa increasingly require deeper drafts and longer berthing spaces to turn around efficiently.

Once completed, Berth 19B will deliver an additional handling capacity of 300,000 TEUs annually.

The expanded capacity is expected to reduce congestion at existing berths, improve vessel turnaround times, and enable smoother cargo evacuation from the port.

The project's scope covers quay wall construction, channel dredging, container yard expansion, and supporting infrastructure designed to integrate seamlessly with existing terminal systems.

Kenya Ports Authority has positioned the development as part of a broader strategy to invest in port infrastructure ahead of demand, rather than in response to it, an approach aimed at reinforcing the Port of Mombasa's standing as the region's preferred logistics and trade hub for the long term.

title: Kwara State Government Strengthens Capacity Building as Eight Procurement Officers Attain Full Membership of APPON
excerpt: All procurement officers at the Kwara State Public Procurement Agency have now attained full membership of the Association of Public Procurement Practitioners of Nigeria, following the induction of eight more officers.
author: Administrator
body:
The Kwara State Public Procurement Agency has recorded another milestone in its drive toward building a highly skilled and professional workforce, as eight additional procurement officers of the Agency were formally inducted into the Association of Public Procurement Practitioners of Nigeria (APPON) during the Association's National Conference held in Enugu State.

With the latest induction, all procurement officers in the Agency have now attained full membership of APPON, underscoring the Kwara State Government's commitment to equipping its workforce with the requisite knowledge, skills, and professional competence needed to deliver efficient and transparent public procurement services.

Speaking on the achievement, the General Manager/Chief Executive Officer of the Agency commended the dedication and hard work of the newly inducted officers.

title: Nigeria Saves $44m on Fertiliser Procurement
excerpt: An early procurement strategy approved by President Bola Tinubu insulated Nigeria from fertiliser price surges triggered by geopolitical tensions, saving the country approximately $44 million.
author: Administrator
body:
Nigeria has saved approximately $44m on fertiliser raw material purchases after the Federal Government approved an early procurement strategy that insulated the country from supply disruptions and price surges triggered by the ongoing conflict between the United States and Iran.

PFI NPK Limited, the implementation vehicle for the Presidential Fertiliser Initiative and a wholly owned entity of the Ministry of Finance Incorporated, disclosed this in a statement issued in Abuja.

The company said the early procurement exercise, approved by President Bola Tinubu, enabled Nigeria to secure fertiliser supplies months before fresh geopolitical tensions disrupted global shipping routes and pushed up the prices of key agricultural inputs.

The strategy generated savings of $43.99m, equivalent to approximately N61.58bn, compared to prevailing international spot market prices.

Director of PFI NPK Limited, Dr Armstrong Takang, said the outcome was the result of deliberate foresight. "We took a deliberate decision to move early, well ahead of market pressures, by securing supply, locking in pricing, and putting the necessary financial instruments in place. That foresight is what has ensured that Nigeria is not exposed to the disruptions currently affecting global fertiliser markets," he said.

To ensure accountability, independent Collateral Management Agents oversee warehouse operations, while raw materials remain under the company's control until sales are completed and repayments made.

Regulatory compliance is enforced through the National Agency for Food and Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC) and the Standards Organisation of Nigeria, with the Office of the National Security Adviser also playing a key role in supporting the programme's nationwide distribution network.

Looking ahead, PFI NPK said it is pursuing long-term supply security through government-to-government partnerships with international suppliers and plans to deploy a digital enterprise platform providing real-time visibility across procurement, inventory management, and distribution operations. Takang said the ultimate objective remains straightforward.

"What matters is that the farmer can access fertilizer when needed and at a price that does not undermine production. By stabilizing supply and managing cost exposure at the procurement stage, we are supporting that outcome at scale," he said.

title: Rainoil Logistics Appoints Oyiadika Odor as Managing Director
excerpt: Rainoil Logistics Services has named Oyiadika Odor as its new Managing Director, capping a career built entirely within the company since 2012 across procurement, depot, and logistics leadership roles.
author: Administrator
body:
Rainoil Logistics Services Limited has announced the appointment of Oyiadika Odor MBF, MNIPR as its new Managing Director.

Odor's journey within Rainoil spans over a decade of hands-on operational experience across multiple functions and geographies.

He began his career with the company as Regional Manager East, before moving through roles including Procurement and Logistics Planning Manager, Depot Head Dispatch, Head of Depot Sales, and Fleet Manager.

He subsequently served as Logistics Manager and then Head of Logistics, a position he held for over four years before his elevation to the Managing Director role.

The appointment marks the elevation of a professional whose entire career has been built within the Rainoil ecosystem, having joined the organisation in 2012 and progressively risen through nearly every level of its logistics and operations structure.

The depth and breadth of that progression, from depot-level operations in the Niger Delta to the leadership of the company's entire logistics arm, reflects a career built not on shortcuts but on institutional knowledge, operational mastery, and sustained performance.

title: Six Continental Group Appoints Benson Mahenya as Group CEO
excerpt: Tanzanian business executive Benson Mahenya has been named Group CEO of Six Continental Group as the company expands its transport, logistics, and trading footprint across East, Central, and Southern Africa.
author: Administrator
body:
Six Continental Group has appointed Tanzanian business executive Benson Mahenya as its new Group Chief Executive Officer, marking a significant step in the company's regional growth strategy.

Mahenya brings more than three decades of experience spanning banking, finance, manufacturing, consulting, governance, and corporate leadership.

His appointment comes as Six Continental Group expands its footprint across East, Central, and Southern Africa in transport, logistics, and trading services.

Before joining the company, Mahenya served as Group CEO of GSM Group of Companies, where he led over 3,000 employees across multiple business sectors.

He previously held key leadership roles within GSM, including Deputy CEO, CEO of the Manufacturing and Trading Division, and Group Chief Financial Officer.

He has also held senior positions at NMB Bank Plc, Standard Chartered Bank Tanzania, BancABC Tanzania, and PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC), building a strong reputation for driving corporate transformation, financial management, and organizational growth.
